methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $1M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 12 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Foundation Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), post-money $3B+ (reported, raise in progress)

data.narrative:
Axial Composites is a Seed-stage company in Robotics & Manufacturing founded by Giuseppe Rapisarda. The company has raised $1M+ to date. The comparable universe includes 9 private peers (including 1X Technologies, Foundation Robotics, Divergent, Exotec, and Locus Robotics) and 1 public comparable (277810.KQ), though the peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or narrow-tier density for reliable median-based valuation.

Given data constraints, Stage-Progression methodology is the primary applicable approach. Using the $1M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.5x step-up multiplier typical of Seed-to-next-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $2M. This method assumes Axial's progression follows standard cadence and capital efficiency patterns observed across early-stage robotics and manufacturing companies.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $2.1M to $2.9M, with a median of $2.5M. This range reflects modest upside and downside variance around the stage-progression baseline. Key uncertainties include the absence of direct peer comparables at Axial's stage—limiting cross-validation—and reliance on total raised as a proxy for intrinsic value rather than operational metrics. Revenue, customer traction, and technology differentiation data would strengthen conviction in this range. A future round with external benchmarking against similar-stage deals in the sector would materially improve valuation precision.
